risk_of_bias.support_text: Summary: The paper states that participants were "randomized blindly to two groups" and later that they were "randomly allocated" to experimental or control groups, but it does not describe how the allocation sequence was generated. The review characteristics also record "Randomisation: no details provided." Because "randomized" alone is not enough to confirm a truly random sequence, the method cannot be judged as low risk.

Summary: The report states that participants were "randomized blindly to two groups" and that caregivers were "randomly allocated" to experimental or control groups, but it gives no explicit description of how the allocation was concealed from the person enrolling participants. The review characteristics also note "Randomisation: no details provided." Because there is no stated mechanism such as central allocation, sequentially numbered opaque sealed envelopes, or identical containers, allocation concealment cannot be confirmed.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Summary: The intervention was an obvious psycho-educational program delivered in 10 face-to-face sessions, while the control group received routine care only. The paper only states that “the co-researcher (assessor) was blind to study treatment and condition,” which refers to outcome assessment, not participants or personnel. Comment: Because participants/caregivers and the clinicians delivering the program would have known the allocation, and the outcomes included subjective measures (patient psychiatric symptoms on BPRS and caregiver burden) that could plausibly be influenced by this knowledge, performance bias is at high risk.

Quote: "The co-researcher (assessor) was blind to study treatment and condition and completed the scales." Comment: Outcome assessment was performed by a blinded co-researcher, which directly addresses detection bias. The main patient outcome here was the BPRS, a clinician-rated scale completed by the blinded assessor, and the caregiver burden questionnaire was also a scale-based outcome, so knowledge of allocation was unlikely to influence measurement.

Quote: "A total of 65 families completed the study. Five participants (two from the experimental group and three from the control group) dropped out before completion of the study for different reasons unrelated to the study." Comment: Attrition was small overall (5/70; 7.1%) and balanced between groups (2 vs 3), with reasons stated as unrelated to the study. This makes bias from missing outcome data unlikely.
